nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.content:
NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
Tier 2 5.4-1 Revision 55.4 Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
The reactor pressure vess el (RPV) of each integrated NuScal e Power Module (NPM) contains the 
reactor and reactor vessel intern als; a pressurizer; two steam ge nerators (SGs); reactor safety 
valves (RSVs); emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves; reactor coolant system (RCS) 
injection, discharge, pressurizer spray, and high-point degasification vent lines; and a decay 
heat removal system (DHRS).
The design basis and description of the reactor and reactor vessel internals are provided in 
Chapter 4. The design basis and description of the RSVs are pr ovided in Section 5.2.2 and the 
design basis and description of the ECCS valves  (i.e., reactor vent va lves (RVVs) and reactor 
recirculation valves (RRVs)) are provid ed in Section 6.3 and Section 5.2.2. 
5.4.1 Steam Generators
The steam generator system (SGS)  consists of: the FW piping from the CNTS to the feed 
plenum access port; thermal relief valve; feed  plenum access port an d access cover; steam 
generators (SGs); steam plenum cap; steam pl enum access port and access cover; and MS 
piping from the steam plenum access port to the CNTS. The FW plenum is within the feed 
plenum access port with the tube sheet fo rming the boundary between the primary and 
secondary side. The MS plenum is within th e RPV integral steam plenum shell with the 
steam plenum cap and RPV integral baffle  plate forming the bo undary between the 
primary and secondary side. The FW piping, ther mal relief valve, steam plenum access port, 
and MS piping of the SGS form th e secondary side of the SGS. 
The SGs in the NPM are integral to the RPV. The RPV forms the SG shell and provides the 
outer pressure boundary of the SGs. The stea m generator tube, tube-to-tubesheet welds, 
and tubesheets provide part of the reactor co olant pressure bounda ry (RCPB). Refer to 
Section 5.2 and Section 5.3 for description and design basis informat ion regarding the RPV 
and the RCPB.
5.4.1.1 Design Basis
The SGs transfer sensible heat from the RC S to the secondary steam system and supply 
superheated steam to the steam and power conversion cycle as described in Chapter 10. 
Table 5.4-1 provides a summary of the oper ating conditions fo r thermal-hydraulic 
design of the SGs. The secondary plant pa rameters represent fu ll-power steam flow 
conditions at the outlet of the steam pl enums at best estima te primary coolant 
conditions.
The SGs provide sufficient stable flow on th e secondary side of the tubes at operational 
power levels and mass flow rates to prec lude reactor power oscillations that could 
result in exceeding specified acceptable fuel design limits.
The secondary side of each SG tube has a fl ow restriction device that is designed to 
reduce the potential for secondary side density wave oscillations. Density wave 
oscillations result in transient loads on applicable regions of the RCS integral RPV and 
SG. The transient load contributions from this event are consid ered in the ASME

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.content:
NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
Tier 2 5.4-40 Revision 55.4.5.5 Pressurizer Materials
The pressurizer includes the top portion of  the RPV upper shell, the RPV upper head, 
the pressurizer baffle plate, heater bundles, and spray nozzles. The material of the RPV upper shell and upper head is described in  Section 5.2. The pressurizer spray nozzles 
and pressurizer spray nozzle safe ends are described in Section 4.5 and Section 5.2, 
respectively. 
The pressurizer baffle plate is an integral part of one of the RP V upper shell forgings 
and is thus constructed of a Grade 3, Class 2, low alloy steel in acco rdance with material 
specification SA-508 of BPVC, Section II. The upper and lower  surfaces of the baffle 
plate are weld clad with two layers of st ainless steel for corrosion protection in 
accordance with material specification SFA- 5.9 of BPVC, Section II; the first layer being 
Alloy Type ER309L and the second layer being Alloy Type ER308L. Additionally, the 
upper surface of the baffle plate in the regi ons of the SG integral steam plenums is clad 
with nickel-based Alloy 52/152 for compatibility.
The specific materials for the heater bundle assemblies are austenitic stainless steel or 
Ni-Cr-Fe Alloy. Materials for the cover plate, bolting, and heater sheaths are corrosion 
resistant materials that are compatible with  their respective operating environments. 
Materials for the cover plate include austenit ic stainless steel Type 304, Grade F304 and 
nickel-based Alloy 690 (UNS N06690). These ma terials comply with BPVC, Section II, 
Specifications SA-182 and SB-168, respectively . Materials for the heater sheaths include 
austenitic stainless steel Type 304L and Ty pe 316. These materials comply with BPVC, 
Section II, Specification SA-213. The bolt ing material is nickel-based Alloy 750 
(UNS N07718) and complies with BPVC , Section II, Specification SB-637.

nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale reactor pressurizer, specifying the alloys and standards for each component, such as the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, and bolting. It emphasizes the use of corrosion-resistant materials and compliance with ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) specifications to ensure compatibility and durability in the reactor coolant system.

edges.description: Section A discusses how steady state flow parameters are established for different reactor power levels, while Section B describes system component operations that are affected by or set up for these flow conditions, connecting them through the operational states defined in Table 5.1-2.

Section A discusses how steady state flow parameters are established for optimal performance at various power levels, while Section B outlines the design basis and natural circulation flow mechanism of the reactor coolant system, connecting them through the operational flow conditions (e.g., 100% power and minimum design flow) that are compared in Table 5.1-2.

Section A discusses how steady state values for system parameters are established as a function of reactor power, which directly relates to the flow conditions (such as 100% power and minimum design flow) that are described in Table 5.1-2, while Section B provides a general description of system features without specifying these operational conditions.

Section A describes specific operational procedures involving system components like the pressurizer and chemical volume control system, which are part of the broader reactor coolant system design and functions outlined in Section B.

Section A describes the operational steps involving the degasification of the pressurizer (PZR) using the RPV high point degasification line, while Section B specifies that the RCS provides this degasification line for removing noncondensible gases from the PZR during normal and emergency operations, thus Section A provides a specific operational example of the general system capability described in Section B.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's reliance on natural circulation, while Section B specifies particular system features (like degasification and pressure boundary integrity) that support the overall design described in Section A.

Section B discusses the establishment of steady state flow parameters at various power levels, which provides the context and criteria for evaluating the pressurizer's function and design described in Section A under different flow conditions.

Section A describes the design and function of the pressurizer within the reactor coolant system, while Section B outlines operational procedures involving the pressurizer water level and pressure adjustments during system transitions, connecting both sections through the operational use and control of the pressurizer under different flow conditions.

Section A describes the pressurizer's design and function as part of the reactor vessel, while Section B provides the overall design basis of the reactor coolant system, explicitly identifying the pressurizer as a subsystem, thus Section A specifies details for the general system described in Section B.

Section B establishes steady state flow parameters at various power levels, including 100% and minimum design flow, which Section A relies on to ensure overpressure protection and compliance with regulatory codes under those conditions.

Section A outlines regulatory compliance and code requirements for overpressure protection, while Section B describes operational procedures affecting system component states, both of which are connected through the need to ensure that overpressure protection provisions remain effective under varying flow conditions such as those compared in Table 5.1-2.

Section A references compliance with regulatory codes for overpressure protection, which are applied to the design and operation of the reactor coolant system described in Section B, including its flow conditions.

Section B establishes steady state flow parameters at various power levels, including 100% and minimum design flow, which Section A relies on to ensure that operating procedures maintain reactor vessel integrity within pressure-temperature limits under those conditions.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity by maintaining pressure-temperature limits during normal and transient power conditions, while Section B describes system component operations (such as feedwater recirculation and pressure adjustments) that are part of those procedures, connecting the operational details to the procedural requirements.

Section A describes the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity by not exceeding pressure-temperature limits during normal and transient power conditions, while Section B outlines the design basis of the reactor coolant system, which determines the flow conditions (such as 100% power and minimum design flow) that these procedures must address.

Section A outlines the regulatory and code compliance requirements for overpressure protection, while Section B describes the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity under various flow conditions, both of which are connected by the need to maintain pressure-temperature limits during different power and flow scenarios as specified in Table 5.1-2.

Section B discusses how steady state flow parameters, such as those at 100% power and minimum design flow, are established for system optimization, which directly relates to the reactor vessel described in Section A as the component where these flow conditions occur.

Section A describes the reactor vessel's role and configuration within the reactor coolant system, while Section B details operational procedures involving system components (such as the pressurizer and steam generators) that directly affect flow conditions relevant to both 100% power and minimum design flow scenarios.

Section A describes the physical configuration and components of the reactor vessel within the NuScale Power Module, while Section B outlines the design basis and operational principles of the reactor coolant system, explicitly connecting the reactor vessel as a key component in both sections.
Section B establishes steady state parameter values for different reactor power levels, which are implemented in the component and subsystem designs described in Section A.

Section A outlines the design and components of the reactor coolant system, establishing the basis for operational conditions such as 100% power flow and minimum design flow, while Section B describes specific operational procedures and system states that occur under varying flow conditions, thus providing context for how the design accommodates these scenarios.

Section A provides detailed descriptions of the reactor coolant system components and their design, while Section B gives a general overview of the system's function and configuration, with both sections connecting through their discussion of the RCS's reliance on natural circulation flow and the components involved.
